articles.label: Government
articles.headline: Escambia County's $47 Million Stormwater Problem Has No Fix in Sight
articles.dek: Three years after voters approved a stormwater fee, the county has spent $12 million and completed exactly one major project. Where's the rest of the money going?
brief: Escambia County has collected $47 million in stormwater fees since 2023 but completed only one major drainage project
Administrative costs, engineering studies and staff salaries consume nearly 40% of annual revenue
County officials say federal permitting delays and contractor shortages have stalled six shovel-ready projects
articles.body:
The drainage ditch behind Marcus Williams's house on Doolittle Road hasn't been cleaned in four years. When it rains hard—and in Pensacola it always rains hard—the water backs up into his yard, pools against his foundation and seeps into his garage.

"They told me when I started paying that fee that things would get better," Williams said, standing in his driveway on a recent Tuesday afternoon. A waterline on his garage door marked the high point from last month's storms. "That was three years ago. Nothing's changed."

Williams is one of roughly 115,000 property owners in unincorporated Escambia County who have paid a stormwater utility fee since November 2023, when voters narrowly approved a referendum creating the dedicated funding stream. The average single-family homeowner pays $7.50 per month. Commercial properties pay based on impervious surface area.

The math suggested progress. At full collection, the fee generates approximately $16 million annually. Over three fiscal years, the county has taken in $47.2 million, according to budget documents obtained through a public records request.

The county has spent $12.1 million.

One major project—the $4.8 million Scenic Highway outfall replacement—has reached completion. Six others remain in various stages of planning, design or permitting limbo. Meanwhile, the unspent balance sits in a restricted fund, growing incrementally while drainage complaints pile up in the county's 311 system.

Where the Money Goes

County Administrator Wes Moreno defended the pace of spending during a budget workshop earlier this month.

"You cannot snap your fingers and make infrastructure appear," Moreno told commissioners. "This is a decade-long commitment. We're building capacity."

Building capacity has proven expensive. Budget documents show that in fiscal year 2025, the stormwater utility spent $2.1 million on personnel—nine full-time positions including engineers, project managers and administrative staff. Another $1.4 million went to outside engineering firms for studies, surveys and design work. Vehicle purchases, equipment and office supplies accounted for $380,000.

Direct construction spending: $3.2 million.

Put differently, for every dollar the county collected in stormwater fees last year, 38 cents went to overhead. The remaining 62 cents either funded construction or stayed in the bank.

"That ratio is not unusual for a new utility in its startup phase," said Dr. Rebecca Torres, a public finance researcher at the University of West Florida. "But three years in, you'd expect to see that flip. Construction should be 70, 80 percent of expenditures by now."

It isn't.

The Permitting Problem

County officials point to federal permitting as the primary bottleneck. Any project that touches wetlands, navigable waters or federally mapped floodplains requires approval from the Army Corps of Engineers. In Northwest Florida, the Corps' Mobile District handles those permits.

The average wait time for a standard individual permit in the Mobile District exceeded 400 days in 2025, according to Corps data. Nationwide the average is 270 days.

"We have six projects with complete engineering packages sitting on someone's desk in Mobile," said county engineer Patricia Hightower. "We can't move dirt until we get those letters."

The projects—drainage improvements in Brent, Ensley, Bellview, Cantonment and two sites near Perdido Key—represent $18.7 million in planned construction. All six received design approval from the county commission between March 2024 and August 2025. None have broken ground.

Hightower said the county has repeatedly requested expedited review, citing public safety concerns after several homes flooded during Hurricane season 2025. The Corps has not granted any expedited requests.

A spokesperson for the Mobile District declined to comment on specific pending applications but said the office is "working diligently to process all permits in accordance with federal law."

The Contractor Question

Even when permits arrive, finding someone to do the work has proven difficult.

The county issued three separate requests for bids on the Brent Lane culvert replacement project. The first two attracted no responses. The third, in January 2026, drew a single bid from a contractor based in Mobile—at 40 percent above the engineer's estimate.

"The civil construction market in this region is completely saturated," Hightower said. "Every contractor with heavy equipment is booked solid doing hurricane recovery work, military contracts or private development."

Naval Air Station Pensacola alone has more than $200 million in active construction projects. Baptist Health Care is building a $615 million campus in West Pensacola. The Perdido Key resort boom continues unabated.

Stormwater ditches in Ensley cannot compete.

The Politics

Commissioner Steven Barry, whose district includes some of the worst-affected areas, has grown publicly frustrated.

"My constituents didn't vote for a savings account," Barry said during the April 10 commission meeting. "They voted for dry houses. We need to figure out how to deliver."

Barry proposed hiring a third-party program manager to accelerate project delivery—essentially outsourcing the work to a private firm with existing federal relationships and contractor networks. The motion failed 3-2, with commissioners Jeff Bergosh and Mike Kohler citing concerns about adding another layer of overhead.

"We just hired nine people to do this job," Kohler said. "Give them time to do it."

The vote split along familiar lines. Barry and Commissioner Lumon May, who represent majority-minority districts with older infrastructure, pushed for faster action. Bergosh, Kohler and Commissioner Doug Underhill, whose districts skew wealthier and newer, urged patience.

The pattern has repeated across multiple stormwater votes over the past two years.

What's Actually Fixed

The Scenic Highway outfall project, the county's sole completed major work, replaced a failing 48-inch drainage pipe that had caused chronic flooding near the intersection of Scenic Highway and Cervantes Street. Construction took 14 months. The project came in $200,000 under budget.

Residents in the immediate area report noticeable improvement.

"Used to be I couldn't get out of my driveway when it rained," said Dorothy Chen, who lives on Scenic Heights Drive. "Now the water's gone in an hour."

Beyond that single success, the county has completed what it classifies as "maintenance and minor improvements"—ditch cleaning, pipe repairs, inlet replacements—totaling roughly $2.3 million across 47 smaller projects. Most involved work that would have been done regardless of the stormwater fee, using general fund money.

The stormwater utility's own annual report, published in February 2026, acknowledged the slow start. "Initial years focused on establishing organizational capacity, developing a comprehensive project prioritization methodology, and building relationships with regulatory partners," the report stated.

Translation: paperwork.

What Happens Next

The county's five-year stormwater capital plan, adopted in September 2025, identifies $89 million in needed projects. At current spending rates, completing that list would take more than 20 years—assuming no new problems emerge and construction costs remain flat.

Neither assumption is safe.

Climate projections suggest Pensacola will see increased rainfall intensity over the coming decades. Development continues to expand impervious surfaces. The infrastructure ages another year with every delay.

Marcus Williams doesn't need projections. He watches the water rise.

"I pay my fee every month," he said. "I'll keep paying it. But I'm 67 years old. At this rate I'll be dead before they fix my ditch."

He's probably not wrong.

articles.label: Feature
articles.headline: The Last Fish House on Pensacola Beach
articles.dek: Joe Patti's grandson runs the only bait shop left on the island. Developers have made four offers this year. He keeps saying no.
brief: Tommy Patti runs Gulf Side Bait & Tackle, the last remaining bait shop on Pensacola Beach, from the same cinderblock building his grandfather built in 1961
Four separate developers have made purchase offers this year, with the latest bid reaching $4.2 million for the quarter-acre lot on Via de Luna Drive
Patti says the shop clears about $80,000 annually but calls it 'the last real place left on this island' as surrounding parcels transform into condos and resort hotels
articles.body:
The mullet are running at 5:47 a.m. and Tommy Patti is already elbow-deep in a cooler of live shrimp when the first customer pulls into the shell-and-gravel lot. A pickup truck. Rod holders bolted to the bed rails. The driver doesn't need to say what he's after.

"Half pound of bull minnows, two dozen shrimp, and whatever that man's having," the driver says, nodding toward a sunburned guy in Crocs filling a Styrofoam cup from the coffee pot Patti keeps on a card table by the door.

This is how it goes at Gulf Side Bait & Tackle. Has been for 65 years.

The cinderblock building sits on a quarter-acre lot at the corner of Via de Luna Drive and Avenida 18, about 200 yards from the Gulf. Patti's grandfather, Joe Patti Sr., poured the foundation himself in 1961, back when Pensacola Beach was mostly dunes and fishing camps and a two-lane road that flooded every time it rained hard. The hand-painted sign out front—a leaping tarpon, scales flaking—dates to 1978. The chest freezer full of frozen cigar minnows is older than Patti himself.

He is 52. Thick hands. Salt-stiff hair that probably used to be brown. He bought the shop from his uncle in 2003 for $180,000, which seemed like a lot of money at the time.

"I could sell this place tomorrow and never work again," Patti said, leaning against the bait tank. "That's what my wife keeps telling me. That's what everybody keeps telling me."

Four developers have made offers this year. The first came in January—$2.8 million from a Destin-based group that builds boutique hotels. Patti said no. In February, a Birmingham investment fund offered $3.1 million, cash, 30-day close. No. March brought a handwritten letter from a man in Mobile who said he wanted to build "something tasteful" and would Patti consider $3.4 million. No.

The fourth offer arrived two weeks ago. A lawyer from a Miami-based hospitality company sat in Patti's shop for 45 minutes, drinking coffee from a Styrofoam cup, admiring the taxidermied cobia on the wall. He left a folder on the counter. Inside: a letter of intent for $4.2 million.

"I told him I'd think about it," Patti said. "I didn't think about it."

What the island lost

Drive Via de Luna from the Bob Sikes Bridge to Fort Pickens Road and count the bait shops. You'll get to one. This one.

Twenty years ago there were five. Pensacola Beach Bait & Tackle closed in 2009, replaced by a condo tower called The Portofino. Island Outfitters lasted until 2014, when the owner retired and sold to a developer who built a mixed-use complex with a rooftop bar. Captain Dave's hung on until 2019. It's a Margaritaville gift shop now.

The last holdout besides Patti was Sandy's Live Bait, a weathered shack near the public beach access that sold shrimp and cold beer and not much else. Sandy Harrelson ran it for 31 years. She sold in 2022 for $1.9 million. The lot sat empty for two years. Now it's a five-story hotel called The Driftwood, where rooms start at $389 a night.

"Sandy cried when she signed the papers," Patti said. "Called me that night, drunk, said she felt like she'd killed something."

Santa Rosa Island Authority records show commercial property values along Via de Luna have increased 340% since 2015. The authority, which manages development on the island under a lease from Escambia County, approved 14 major commercial projects between 2020 and 2025. Twelve of them replaced existing structures. Nine of those structures had been locally owned businesses.

"The economics are just brutal," said Dr. Richard Burdeshaw, an economist at the University of West Florida who studies coastal development. "You've got a guy running a bait shop, clearing maybe six figures in a good year, sitting on land worth several million dollars. The math doesn't make sense for him to stay. Except sometimes people don't make decisions based on math."

What the numbers say

Patti let me look at his books. He didn't have to. He wanted to.

Gulf Side Bait & Tackle grossed $247,000 in 2025. After costs—bait suppliers, utilities, the one part-time employee he hires during tourist season, maintenance on a building that seems to need something fixed every month—he cleared about $80,000.

His property tax bill last year was $14,200, up from $8,100 in 2020. The Santa Rosa Island Authority's ground lease costs him another $6,400 annually. Insurance—flood, wind, liability—runs $23,000.

"I'm not getting rich," he said. "Never did. Never expected to."

The $4.2 million offer would net him roughly $3.8 million after taxes and fees, according to a back-of-napkin calculation he did with his accountant. Invested conservatively, that's $150,000 a year in income without touching the principal.

"My accountant thinks I'm crazy," Patti said. "My wife thinks I'm stubborn. My kids think I'm both."

He has two kids. A daughter in Tallahassee, 28, works for the state. A son in Birmingham, 25, just started law school. Neither wants the shop.

"I asked them," Patti said. "They said no. Can't blame them. This isn't a life most people want anymore."

What the regulars say

By 7 a.m. the parking lot is full. A dozen trucks and SUVs, most with boat trailers attached. The coffee pot is empty. Patti makes another.

The regulars know each other. They know Patti. They know where the redfish are hitting and whether the specs have moved into the pass yet and who caught what last weekend. This is the information economy of a bait shop—free, accurate, updated in real time.

"You can't get this at Bass Pro," said Gene Hutchins, 67, a retired firefighter from Cantonment who's been coming here since the 1980s. "You walk in there, kid behind the counter doesn't know a pompano from a pinfish. Here, Tommy knows. His daddy knew. His granddaddy knew."

Hutchins buys a bucket of live shrimp and a pack of circle hooks. Total: $18.50. He's been coming here twice a week for 40 years. That's roughly 4,000 visits. Maybe $75,000 in lifetime purchases, he figures.

"If this place closes, I don't know where I go," Hutchins said. "Drive to Pensacola, I guess. Joe Patti's on the mainland. But it's not the same."

Joe Patti's Seafood—the famous fish market on West Main Street—is a distant cousin operation, started by Tommy's great-uncle. Same family, different branches. Tommy's grandfather split off in the 1950s, moved to the beach, built this place.

"People always ask if we're related," Patti said. "We are. But we're not, you know? They're over there. We're over here. Different thing."

What the developers want

The Miami company that made the $4.2 million offer is called Coastal Hospitality Partners. Their portfolio includes 14 hotels across Florida, mostly in the $200-to-400-a-night range. Boutique properties. Rooftop bars. Farm-to-table restaurants. The kind of places that describe themselves as "elevated" and "curated."

A spokesperson for the company, reached by phone, declined to discuss the specific offer but confirmed their interest in Pensacola Beach.

"We see tremendous potential in that market," said the spokesperson, Amanda Chen. "The island is undergoing a transformation, and we'd like to be part of that story."

Asked what kind of development they envision for Patti's lot, Chen said: "It's premature to discuss specifics. But I can say our properties are designed to complement their surroundings. We're not about disruption. We're about enhancement."

Patti laughed when I read him that quote.

"Enhancement," he said. "That's a good one. You know what they'd build? Same thing everybody builds. Six stories, infinity pool, bar on the roof. They'd call it something like 'The Fisherman' or 'The Angler' and put a fake marlin on the wall. Enhancement."

What stays

The cobia on Patti's wall is real. His grandfather caught it in 1974, off the old Pensacola Beach pier before Hurricane Ivan took it. Sixty-three pounds. The mount cost $200, which was a lot in 1974.

The photos on the wall behind the register are real too. Black-and-white shots of the shop in the 1960s, when the road out front was unpaved. Color shots from the 1980s, a young Tommy Patti standing next to his father, both holding king mackerel. A faded Polaroid of Hurricane Ivan's aftermath in 2004, the building still standing, water up to the windowsills.

"We've been through every storm," Patti said. "Ivan, Dennis, Sally. The building's still here. I'm still here."

He's thought about what happens when he can't run the place anymore. He's 52. His knees hurt in the morning. His back goes out a couple times a year. The 4 a.m. wake-ups are harder than they used to be.

"I figure I've got maybe 10, 15 years left in me," he said. "After that, I don't know. Maybe I sell then. Maybe I just lock the door and walk away. Let the building fall down. Let the land go back to the island."

He paused. Looked out the window at the parking lot, the trucks, the trailers, the morning light coming in off the Gulf.

"Or maybe I don't," he said. "Maybe I just keep saying no."

The coffee pot is empty again. He makes another.

articles.label: Military
articles.headline: NAS Pensacola Expects 200 New Training Slots as Navy Expands Pilot Pipeline
articles.dek: The base will see its largest class increase since 2019, bringing more students and more strain on off-base housing already at capacity.
brief: Naval Air Station Pensacola will add approximately 200 student aviator slots beginning in October, the largest single-year increase since 2019.
The expansion responds to Navy-wide pilot shortages but arrives as Pensacola's rental market posts a 2.1% vacancy rate, well below the national average.
Base officials say they're working with local landlords and property managers but acknowledge most students will compete for housing in an already tight market.
articles.body:
The T-6B Texan IIs lift off from Sherman Field most mornings by 7 a.m., banking south over Bayou Grande before circling back for touch-and-go landings. By October, those patterns will get more crowded.

Naval Air Station Pensacola is adding approximately 200 student aviator training slots in fiscal year 2027, base officials confirmed this week. The increase represents the largest single-year expansion of the primary flight training pipeline since 2019, when the Navy began ramping up production to address chronic pilot shortages across the fleet.

"This is a reflection of the Navy's commitment to building the aviator force we need," said Capt. Timothy Kinsella, NAS Pensacola's commanding officer, during a brief media availability Tuesday. "Pensacola has always been the cradle of naval aviation. We're prepared to meet this challenge."

The expansion will bring the base's student aviator population to roughly 1,400 at any given time, up from the current 1,200. Students typically spend six to eight months at NAS Pensacola completing primary flight training before moving to advanced training at bases in Texas, Mississippi, or Florida's Whiting Field.

Housing pressure builds

The new students won't live on base. NAS Pensacola's bachelor officer quarters reached capacity in 2023, and the Navy has no plans for new construction. That means 200 additional renters entering a market that property managers describe as already stretched thin.

Pensacola's rental vacancy rate sat at 2.1% in the first quarter of 2026, according to data from the University of West Florida's Haas Center. The national average hovers around 6.5%. A healthy market typically runs between 5% and 8%.

"We're already turning away qualified applicants every week," said Marcus Webb, a property manager with Gulf Coast Realty who oversees 340 rental units across Pensacola and Gulf Breeze. "When I hear 200 more coming, I don't know where they're supposed to go."

Student aviators are commissioned officers, typically ensigns or second lieutenants, drawing base pay of roughly $3,600 per month plus a housing allowance. The 2026 Basic Allowance for Housing rate for an O-1 without dependents in Pensacola is $1,371 per month.

That allowance hasn't kept pace with rents. The median asking rent for a one-bedroom apartment in Pensacola hit $1,285 in March, according to Zillow data. Two years ago it was $1,090.

"The math doesn't work like it used to," said Lt. j.g. Rebecca Torres, a flight instructor at Training Squadron Six. Torres completed her own primary training at Pensacola in 2022. "I had roommates. Everyone has roommates now."

Base and city coordination

Kinsella said base leadership has been meeting with local property management companies and the Pensacola Association of Realtors to discuss the incoming students. He declined to characterize those conversations as negotiations.

"We're making sure the community understands what's coming and when," Kinsella said. "These are good tenants. They have steady income, they're responsible, and they're temporary. That should be attractive to landlords."

Pensacola City Council member Jared Moore, whose District 6 includes neighborhoods near the base, said he learned of the expansion through media reports rather than direct communication from the Navy.

"I would have appreciated a heads-up," Moore said. "We're already dealing with short-term rental pressures, affordability issues, traffic. Another 200 people looking for apartments affects more than just the base."

Moore said he plans to request a briefing from base officials at the council's May 8 meeting.

Training demands drive expansion

The pilot shortage driving these numbers isn't new. The Navy has acknowledged a shortfall of roughly 1,400 pilots since 2018, driven by a combination of increased commercial airline hiring, retention challenges, and training bottlenecks created during COVID-19 shutdowns.

NAS Pensacola's primary training operation—run by Training Air Wing Five—graduated 1,847 student aviators in fiscal year 2025. The Navy's goal for FY2027 is 2,100 graduates across all primary training sites, with Pensacola expected to produce more than half.

Reaching that number requires more than just slots. It requires aircraft availability, instructor pilots, simulator time, and clear weather. Pensacola's subtropical climate provides more flyable days than most alternatives, which is why the base has anchored naval aviation training since 1914.

"You can't just wish more pilots into existence," said retired Rear Adm. James Carroll, a former Training Air Wing Five commander who now teaches at the Naval War College. "The infrastructure has to support the throughput. Pensacola has that infrastructure, but every system has limits."

What comes next

The first wave of additional students is expected to report in October. Base officials say the ramp-up will be gradual, with full capacity reached by early 2027.

For landlords, that timeline offers little runway. Webb said his company has already seen an uptick in inquiries from relocation specialists working with incoming military families, though student aviators typically handle their own housing searches.

"We'll figure it out," Webb said. "We always do. But somebody's going to be paying $1,500 for a place that rented for $1,100 three years ago. That's just reality."

Torres, the flight instructor, offered a more optimistic view.

"Pensacola's a Navy town," she said. "It has been for a hundred years. The community adjusts. The students adjust. And eventually, we all get our wings and move on."

She paused.

"But yeah. Get a roommate."
